coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots/Zn2+ ions doped-CdS nanowires with enhanced photocatalytic activity for reduction of 4-nitroaniline to p-phenylenediamine
coredata.dc:creator: Chai, Yuan-Yuan
Qu, De-Peng
Ma, De-Kun
Chen, Wei
Huang, Shaoming
coredata.dc:description: It was widely accepted that pristine CdS showed weak photocatalytic activity for reduction of 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) to p-phenylenediamine (PPD) because of its poor photogenerated carriers separation and transfer efficiency. Yet the influence of conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) redox potentials on its photocatalytic reduction reaction efficiency was often neglected, especially the VB potential. Considering the above facts, in this work, we design a highly efficient carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Zn2+ ions doped-CdS nanowires (ZnCdS NWs) composite photocatalysts, where CQDs were acted as metal-free co-catalysts to promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers and Zn2+ ions doping was used to adjust the redox of CdS NWs. As a result, under visible light irradiation, 4-NA could be completely converted to PPD with near 100% selectivity over the as-synthesized CQDs/ZnCdS NWs within 6 min in the presence of ammonium formate. It was found that Zn2+ ions doping increased VB potential of CdS rather than lowering its CB potential, which was responsible for its enhanced photocatalytic activity. The present strategy on the combination of co-catalyst and energy band engineering could open a new avenue to develop highly efficient visible-light-driven photocatalysts for green organic synthesis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdS nanowires
Carbon quantum dots
Doping
4-Nitroaniline
Photocatalytic reduction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs/ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/CdS NWs
Electrochemical impendence spectra, Mott-Schottky, and photoelectrochemical measurements

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China for Distinguished Young Scholars under Grant No. LR16B010002 and the National Science Foundation of China ( 51372173 , 21673160 and 51420105002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM images of the as-synthesized CdS NWs (a), ZnCdS NWs (b), and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs (c). TEM image of CQDs (d). HRTEM image of CQDs/ZnCdS NWs (e).
(a) XRD patterns of the as-obtained CdS NWs, ZnCdS NWs, and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs. (b) Magnified diffraction peak corresponds to (102) crystal plane for the same samples.

(a) Time evolution of UV–vis absorption spectra of 4-NA aqueous solution over CQDs/ZnCdS NWs under visible light irradiation. (b) Concentration changes of 4-NA as a function of irradiation time over CdS NWs, CQDs/CdS NWs, ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs, respectively.

UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and plots (b) of (αhν)2 versus hν of CdS NWs, ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs, respectively. Mott-Schottky plots of CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs (c). Energy band structures of CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs and redox potentials of 4-NA/PPD and HCO2 –/CO2 – (d).
DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs water suspensions in the presence of ammonium formate under visible light irradiation.

PL spectra (a), linear sweep voltammetry curves under chopped visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) (b), photocurrent-time curves of ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs under chopped visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) (c), and EIS spectra of CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs measured under visible light illumination and in the dark (d).
Schematic illustration for photocatalytic reduction reaction mechanism of 4-NA over CQDs/ZnCdS NWs in the presence of ammonium formate under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots/Zn2+ ions doped-CdS nanowires with enhanced photocatalytic activity for reduction of 4-nitroaniline to p-phenylenediamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325027, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It was widely accepted that pristine CdS showed weak photocatalytic activity for reduction of 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) to p-phenylenediamine (PPD) because of its poor photogenerated carriers separation and transfer efficiency. Yet the influence of conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) redox potentials on its photocatalytic reduction reaction efficiency was often neglected, especially the VB potential. Considering the above facts, in this work, we design a highly efficient carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Zn2+ ions doped-CdS nanowires (ZnCdS NWs) composite photocatalysts, where CQDs were acted as metal-free co-catalysts to promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers and Zn2+ ions doping was used to adjust the redox of CdS NWs. As a result, under visible light irradiation, 4-NA could be completely converted to PPD with near 100% selectivity over the as-synthesized CQDs/ZnCdS NWs within 6 min in the presence of ammonium formate. It was found that Zn2+ ions doping increased VB potential of CdS rather than lowering its CB potential, which was responsible for its enhanced photocatalytic activity. The present strategy on the combination of co-catalyst and energy band engineering could open a new avenue to develop highly efficient visible-light-driven photocatalysts for green organic synthesis.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, visible-light-driven photocatalysis for selective organic transformations has received dramatic renaissance of interest because of its low cost, easy availability, environmental friendliness, and unique reaction mechanism [1–6]. At present, the used photocatalysts for organic reaction can be mainly classified in four categories: metal complexes, dyes, plasmonic metal nanoparticles, and semiconductors [7]. Among them, metal complexes and dyes possess good photocatalytic activity. However, the former usually contains expensive noble metal elements such as ruthenium and iridium, while the latter is unstable and easy to be self-degraded through photosensitized process [8]. In addition, homogenous reaction system makes the separation of the two kinds of photocatalysts from the solution inconvenient. For plasmonic metal nanoparticles, synthesizing specific sizes and/or shapes are often required in order to obtain high photocatalytic efficiency [9]. Comparatively speaking, semiconductor-type photocatalysts can overcome the above-mentioned weaknesses because of their low cost, good photostability, tunable energy band structure, and heterogeneous reaction system, which have received increasing attention [10]. Therefore it is highly desirable to develop efficient visible-light-responding semiconductor photocatalysts for green organic synthesis.

PPD is a kind of important chemical raw material and industrial intermediate, which has been widely used in the production of various dyes [11], polymers [12], developing agents [13], rubber antioxidants [14], and aramid textile fibers [15]. PPD is generally produced by the selective hydrogenation of 4-NA over metal, especially noble metal catalysts such as Au, Pt, Ru and Pd under hydrogen at a relatively high temperature and a certain pressure [16]. However, the above methods are subjected to rigorous reaction conditions, high cost and tedious procedures [17]. Recently, it has been reported that 4-NA could be direct reduced to PPD in water under visible light irradiation over several typical photocatalysts such as CdS [18], CdS-based composites [19], PbBi2Nb2O9 [20], and In2S3 [21]. This kind of visible-light-driven synthetic method has attracted considerable attention because it conforms the standard of green synthesis. However, the photocatalytic conversion efficiency of 4-NA to PPD is not yet ideal for practical application.

Compared with bulky CdS, CdS NWs can show better photoctalytic activity because of their unique geometrical advantages such as large specific surface area, facile electron-hole pairs transport and separation, and enhanced light absorption and scattering ability [22]. Various strategies have been developed to improve the photocatalytic activity of pristine CdS NWs including loading of co-catalysts [23], coupling with energy band matched semiconductors or conductive nanostructured carbon materials [24,25], forming solid solution [26], combined with biocatalysts [27], constructing multi-component photocatalytic system [28]. However, the above modified CdS NWs were mainly used to photocatalytic water splitting into H2. In contrast, the studies on CdS NWs-based photocatalysts for photocatalytic reduction of 4-NA to PPD are still at the primary stage. Recently, Xu group has constructed a series of highly efficient CdS NWs-based composite photocatalysts for photocatalytic reduction of 4-NA [29]. The enhanced photocatalytic activity for the materials was mainly ascribed to efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers through the heterojunction [30]. Wu and co-authors found that the band structure of CdS had obvious influence on the reduction efficiency of 4-NA and its VB potential also played a key role during the chemical conversion process of 4-NA [31]. Yu et al. reported that Zn2+ ions doping can lead to the shift of the valence band of CdS to the direction with high oxidative ability [32]. The resultant ZnxCd1−xS showed enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB. However, the photocatalytic reduction of 4-NA over ZnxCd1−xS has never been reported.

As a new member of nanostructured carbon materials, CQDs have attracted considerable attention because of their unique structure, low cytotoxity, ease of surface functionalization, and good photoinduced electron transfer ability and tunable light-harvesting range [33]. In the aspect of photocatalysis, they played varied roles such as photocatalysts [34], co-catalysts [35], and photosensitizers [36]. Unlike carbon nanotubes and graphene, CQDs are easy to be combined with other photocatalysts through simple adsorption [37]. On the other hand, recently, it has been reported that nanostructured carbon materials could show excellent catalytic activity for the reduction of nitrobenzene [38,39]. CQDs are expected to exhibit excellent surface catalytic activity due to large surface to bulk atomic ratio. Therefore they are very suitable for employing as metal-free co-catalysts to boost the photocatalytic activity of ZnxCd1−xS NWs for reduction of 4-NA.

Based on the above considerations, in this work, visible-light-responding CQDs/ZnCdS NWs composite photocatalysts were synthesized through a mild solution-phase route. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ZnCdS NWs for reduction of 4-NA to PPD was systematically compared with that of CdS NWs, CQDs/CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs. The influences of Zn2+ ions doping on the energy band position and photocatalytic activity of CdS NWs were investigated. The role of CQDs as co-catalyst was revealed on the basis of a series of technique characterizations such as photoluminescence (PL) spectra, photoelectrochemical tests, electrochemical impendence spectra (EIS), and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra. The experimental results showed that the synergistic effect between CQDs loading and Zn2+ ions doping was attributed to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ZnCdS NWs. The present study provides a new strategy for designing highly efficient photocatalysts for green organic synthetic chemistry.

CQDs were synthesized through a modified molecular fusion route under hydrothermal conditions [40]. In a typical process, 0.2 g of pyrene and 20 mL of HNO3 were added into a three-necked flask and refluxed at 80 °C for 12 h. After cooled to room temperature, the resultant yellow solid was separated by centrifugation, washed by ultrapure water and absolute ethanol for several times, then dried at 60 °C under vacuum. After that, 0.2 g of the as-obtained powders were dispersed into 40 mL of NaOH solution (0.2 M) by ultrasonication. The suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave with 50 mL of capacity and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooled to room temperature, the solution was dialysed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da). The final solid CQDs powders were obtained through freeze drying.

The synthesis of CdS NWs was similar to our previous report [24]. Typically, 1 mmol of Cd(OAc)2⋅2H2O and 2 mmol of NaS2CNEt2⋅3H2O were dissolved into a mixture of ethylenediamine (34 mL) and dodecanethiol (6 mL) under stirring. Then the solution was put into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (50 mL of capacity) and maintained at 180 °C for 48 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the products were collected via centrifugation, washed with alcohol and ultrapure water for several times, and then dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 4 h.

0.5 mmol of the as-obtained CdS NWs and 0.5 mmol of Zn(NO3)2⋅5H2O were dispersed into 40 mL of ethylene glycol under stirring. Then the solution was put into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (50 mL of capacity) and maintained at 160 °C for 6 h. The post treatment process of the products was the same as to that of CdS NWs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs/ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/CdS NWs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical process, 20 mg of CQDs were dispersed into 100 mL of ultrapure water. Then 200 µL of the solution was drawn off through a syringe and injected into 10 mL of ultrapure water containing 80 mg of ZnCdS NWs. The solution was stirred for 6 h at room temperature. After that, the post treatment process of the products was the same as to that of CdS NWs. For the preparation of CQDs/CdS NWs, it is the same as that of CQDs/ZnCdS NWs except that ZnCdS NWs were replaced by CdS NWs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were performed on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometre using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) at scanning rate of 8°/min in the 2θ range of 10–70°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out with a Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an excitation source of Al Kα radiation (λ = 1253.6 eV). Raman spectrum was measured on a confocal Raman microscopic system (HORIBA iHR550) with excitation light of 532 nm, using Si/SiO2 as sample support. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were obtained on a Nova NanoSEM 200 with 15 kV as the excitation voltage. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, high-resolution electron microscopy (HRTEM) images, and elemental mapping images were taken on a JEOL 2010 microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) were recorded on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–Vis spectrometer with an integrating sphere using BaSO4 as the reference. UV–Vis DRS can be converted to corresponding absorption spectra through the Kubelka-Monk function. Inductively coupled plasma (ICP) measurements were conducted on a VIST-MAX inductive coupling plasma emission spectrograph. The measurement of C element was performed on a PE 2400II CHNS/O elemental analyzer. 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) spin-trapping ESR spectra were performed at a JES FA200 (JEOL) spectrometer at room temperature under visible light illumination (λ > 420 nm). Main parameters are set as follows: microwave frequency, 9054.487 MHz; microwave power, 0.998 mW; center field, 323.53 mT; sweep width, 5 mT. PL spectra were carried out on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA JobinYvon Inc.) equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impendence spectra, Mott-Schottky, and photoelectrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: All measurements were performed with a standard three-electrode configuration on a CHI760E electrochemical system, using the as-obtained CdS NWs, ZnCdS NWs, CQDs/ZnCdS NWs coated F-doped FTO glass (1 cm × 2 cm) as the working electrode, respectively, a Pt wire as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. For electrochemical impendence spectra (EIS), they are measured under illumination and in the dark at open circuit potential with an amplitude of 5 mV in the range of 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz, respectively, using 0.5 M Na2SO4 as electrolyte. To Mott-Schottky plots, they are performed at 1000 Hz within potential range from 1.0 V to −0.8 V versus reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). As for the photocurren-time curves, they are carried out at 0 V versus RHE. For easy comparisons, all the electrode potentials were converted to RHE scale, using the following Nernst equation: E(RHE) = E(SCE) + 0.242 + 0.059 × pH.

In a typical process, 40 mg of ammonium formate was dissolved into 30 mL of 4-NA aqueous solution (20 mg L−1) in a quartz vial. Then 20 mg of the catalysts were dispersed in the solution under stir. After that, the solution was agitated in the dark for 6 h to ensure the establishment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the catalysts and reactants. Subsequently, the solution was saturated by N2 and then sealed by ground glass stopper. Afterward, the solution was irradiated under a 500 W Xe lamp equipped with a cutoff filter of 420 nm. At a certain time interval, 3 mL of the solution was collected and centrifuged to remove the catalysts. Finally, the solution was analyzed on a Shimadzu UV2450 PC spectrophotometer.

Fig. 1 a–c represents panoramic FE-SEM images of the as-synthesized products. As shown in Fig. 1a, uniform CdS NWs could be obtained on a large scale through the facile solvothermal route. The morphology of CdS NWs has no obvious change after Zn2+ ions doping (Fig. 1b). The loading of CQDs does not affect the morphology and dispersity of ZnCdS NWs (Fig. 1c), either. Fig. 1d shows a typical TEM image of the as-prepared CQDs. These CQDs have a size less than 5 nm and show strong green fluorescence under excitation of 365 nm wavelength (Supporting Information, Fig. S1), which is in agreement with previous report [40]. CQDs could firmly adsorb on the surface of ZnCdS NWs because of their small sizes and strong electrostatic interaction between CQDs and ZnCdS NWs [41], which could be further supported by adsorption test (Supporting Information). It could be clearly seen that the color of the solution obviously bleached after the introduction of ZnCdS NWs into the original CQDs solution (Fig. S2). The above results showed that CQDs were efficiently adsorbed on the surfaces of ZnCdS NWs. HRTEM image of CQDs/ZnCdS NWs could further confirm this point. As seen from Fig. 1e, the interplane spacing of 0.358 nm is in accordance with (1 0 0) plane of hexagonal-phase CdS, while the areas marked with white elliptical curves correspond to CQDs. These CQDs closely adhered on the surfaces of ZnCdS NWs. As further observed from corresponding elemental mapping images of the sample (Fig. S3), CQDs are uniformly distributed on the surfaces of ZnCdS NWs.

Fig. 2 a shows XRD patterns of CdS NWs, ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs. All the diffraction peaks could be indexed to hexagonal-phase CdS (JCPDS No. 65-3414). For CQDs/ZnCdS NWs sample, XRD peaks of CQDs were not detected due to their low content and poor crystallinity. Zn2+ ions doping did not induce other phases or impurities, either. As previous reports, Zn2+ ions could be incorporated into CdS matrix through substituting for Cd2+ lattice sites under hydrothermal condition [32]. As a result, compared with pristine CdS NWs, the lattice fringe distance of ZnCdS NWs would decrease due to smaller radius of Zn2+ ion (0.74 Å) than that of Cd2+ ion (0.97 Å). From the magnified diffraction peak located at 35–40° (Fig. 2B), it could be clearly seen that the diffraction peak of ZnCdS NWs was shifted to high degree direction, compared with original CdS NWs. This result further showed that Zn2+ ions were doped into the lattice of CdS NWs. It should be pointed out here that the doping of Zn2+ ions and loading of CQDs did not obviously affect the crystallinity of CdS NWs, namely, not inducing new defects. This point is very important because the defects were often considered as recombination center of photogenerated carriers. Raman spectra also show the characteristic Raman bands of ZnxCd1−xS (Fig. S4a) and CQDs (Fig. S4b). The vibrational peaks located at 299 and 602 cm−1 correspond to 1st and 2nd longitudinal-optical phonons in ZnxCd1−xS, respectively, which is in agreement with reported result [42].The vibrational peaks located at 1596 cm−1 and 1382 cm−1 are ascribed to ordered G band and disordered D band of CQDs. The XPS survey spectrum further confirmed that the products consist of Zn, Cd, S, and C elements (Fig. S5a). The two peaks located at 404.2 and 411.0 eV (Fig. S5b) are attributed to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 in ZnCdS, while the binding energies of 1021.2 and 1044.3 eV (Fig. S5c) correspond to Zn 2p3/2 and 2p5/2 in ZnCdS [43]. The positions of the S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 (Fig. S5d) are consistent with reported values of S 2p in ZnCdS [44]. Compared with pure ZnS and CdS, both the binding energies of Zn 2p and Cd 3d are shifted, which also shows efficient doping of Zn2+ ions in CdS lattice. It should be pointed out here that only 1.89% of Zn2+ ions were efficiently doped into the lattice of CdS NWs on the basis of ICP measurements, although molar ratio of CdS NWs to Zn2+ ions source is 1:1, suggesting that it was a surface doping process. On the other hand, calculated from elemental analysis, the content of CQDs in CQDs/ZnCdS NWs composites is ca. 0.21 wt.%. Note worthily, although low Zn2+ ions doping and CQDs loading level, the as-obtained CQDs/ZnCdS NWs showed enhanced photocatalytic activity compared with pristine CdS NWs, as showed by the following photocatalytic reduction reaction of 4-NA.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-obtained products was evaluated toward selective reduction of 4-NA to corresponding PPD in aqueous solution in the presence of ammonium formate under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) under ambient conditions. The characteristic absorption peaks of 4-NA and PPD are located at 382 nm, 240 and 305 nm [45], respectively. Considering the present reaction solution obeys Lambert–Beer law, therefore the concentration changes of 4-NA and PPD in the solution could be monitored through time-dependent UV–Vis absorption spectra by recording variations of their absorbance. Fig. 3 a presents typically temporal evolution of UV–Vis absorption spectra during the photocatalytic reduction of 4-NA over CQDs/ZnCdSNWs photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. As can be seen from Fig. 3a, the concentration of 4-NA fast decreased with prolonged light irradiation time, while the concentration of PPD significantly increased. Within 6 min, the conversion yield of 4-NA into PPD could near reach 100%. When irradiation time was extended, the yield of PPD was not further increased and it did not decrease, either. The photocatalytic activity of CdS NWs, CQDs/CdS NWs, ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs was compared and shown in Fig. 3b. Obviously, both CQDs loading and Zn2+ ions doping enhanced the photocatalytic activity of CdS NWs, while CQDs and Zn2+ ions co-modified CdS NWs showed the best photocatalytic activity. The synergistic effect between CQDs loading and Zn2+ ions doping was ascribed to enhanced photocatalytic activity of CdS NWs for reduction of 4-NA to PPD, which will be discussed in the subsequent section.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, both Zn2+ ions doping and CQDs loading did not extend spectra responsive range of CdS NWs. They also showed similar photoabsorption ability. The badgap energy (Eg) of the samples measured from the (αhν)2−hν curves are almost the same (Fig. 4b), ca. 2.42 eV. In order to investigate the influence of doping of Zn2+ ions on the energy band structure and carrier density of CdS NWs, the Mott-Schottky plots of the electrodes respectively fabricated by CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs were measured. As shown in Fig. 4c, the Mott-Schottky plots of both the electrodes show the positive slopes, indicating n-type semiconductors. In addition, they have similar slopes, suggesting that Zn2+ ions doping did not obviously change carrier density of CdS NWs. However, the flat band potential of ZnCdS NWs shifted toward more positive direction, compared with that of original CdS NWs. The flat potential of CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs were determined to −0.38 and −0.24 V vs. RHE by the extrapolation of the linear portion of the plots, respectively. Considering the flat potential of n-type semiconductor is near to the position of conduction-band bottom (E CB) [46], the valence band-edge potential (E VB) can be determined by E VB = E CB + E g [47]. Therefore the doping of Zn2+ ions increased both E CB and E VB of CdS NW, as shown in Fig. 4d.

According to previous report [31], the reduction potential for 4-NA/PPD redox couple in water is −0.03 V. Therefore the photogenerated electrons on both conduction bands of CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs could reduce 4-NA to PPD in the current photocatalytic reaction system. In theory, 4-NA is easier to be reduced to PPD over CdS NWs than on ZnCdS NWs because the former has more negative E CB, namely, stronger reductive ability than the latter. However, as shown in Fig. 3b, on the contrary, ZnCdS NWs showed better photocatalytic activity for the reduction of 4-NA. Considering CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs have similar photoabsorption capability and adsorption ability for 4-NA (Fig. S6), enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZnCdS NW should be related to its more positive E VB potential. It had been reported that
CO2− radical could efficiently reduce 4-NA to PPD in aqueous solution because of its strong reductive ability ((E(
CO2 –/CO2) = −1.55 V vs. RHE) [48], while the photogenerated holes on both valence bands of CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs have enough positive potentials to drive the oxidation of HCO2– ions within ammonium formate to the active species
CO2– radicals (E(HCO2 –/
CO2 –) = 1.73 V vs. RHE) [49]. In view of higher E VB for ZnCdS NWs, HCO2– ions were easily converted into
CO2– radicals on them. That is, more reductive
CO2– radicals would be produced on ZnCdS NWs than on CdS NWs under visible light irradiation. In order to confirm this point, DMPO spin-trapping ESR measurements were carried out. As seen from Fig. 5 , a sextet of characteristic peaks corresponding to DMPO-
CO2-adduct can be observed both in CdS NWs and ZnCdS NWs water suspensions under visible light irradiation [31]. However, obviously, the ESR signal intensity of ZnCdS NWs is stronger than that of CdS NWs, which showed that more
CO2-radicals were formed over the former. Therefore the enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZnCdS NWs could be attributed to their larger E VB that induced more
CO2– radicals to reduce 4-NA to PPD under the same reaction conditions.
CO2-radicals could efficiently reduce 4-NA, which could be further confirmed by contrastive experiment. Where ammonium formate was replaced by 0.05 M of Na2S and Na2SO3 couple as hole scavengers, the other reaction conditions were kept unchanged. As a result, the photoreduction efficiency of 4-NA obviously decreased over CQDs/ZnCdS NWs due to the absence of
CO2-radicals, as shown in Fig. S7.

On the other hand, CQDs played an important role as co-catalysts in boosting the photocatalytic activity of ZnCdS NWs, as confirmed by subsequent PL, photocurrent and EIS spectra. Fig. 6 a represents typical PL emission spectra of ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs under excitation of 365 nm wavelength. The strong and wide emission peaks located at 525 nm can be ascribed to surface state of CdS [50]. Obviously, the PL emission intensity of CQDs/ZnCdS NWs weakened after the modification of CQDs. This change can be attributed to efficient transfer of photogenerated electrons produced on ZnCdS NWs to CQDs because of large work function of the latter. CQDs promoted the separation of photogenerated carriers, which could be further demonstrated by photocurrent-potential curves for ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs. As shown in Fig. 6b, while both the samples show an anodic photoresponse indicating that they are n-type semiconductors, CQDs/ZnCdS NWs photoelectrode shows larger photocurrent density than ZnCdS NWs one. To be specific, the photocurrent density for CQDs/ZnCdS NWs (5.57 mA·cm−2) is 1.7-times larger than that of pristine ZnCdS NWs (3.26 mA·cm−2) at an applied voltage of 1 V vs. RHE. Especially, CQDs/ZnCdS NWs show larger photocurrent and better stability than ZnCdS NWs at 0 V vs. RHE, as shown by their current-time curves (Fig. 6c). The enhanced photocatalytic activity and photostability of CQDs/ZnCdS NWs are ascribed to CQDs as electron sink that efficiently separates photogenerated carriers. EIS measurements were further employed to evaluate the electronic resistance for charge transfer across the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 6d, CQDs/ZnCdS NWs electrode under visible light irradiation has the smallest diameter of semicircle in the Nyquist plots, indicating that the loading of CQDs on ZnCdS NWs electrode benefits fast charge transfer across the electrode/electrolyte interface. Considering all the above facts, CQDs played a role as metal-free co-catalysts in CQDs/ZnCdS NWs composite photocatalysts.

Based on the above experimental results, the photocatalytic reduction reaction mechanism of 4-NA over CQDs/ZnCdS NWs in the presence of ammonium formate can be proposed as follows. After CQDs/ZnCdS NW was excited by visible light, photogenerated electrons would be transited from its VB to CB and holes be left on the VB. Immediately, electrons were transferred to the surfaces of CQDs and participated in subsequent reduction reaction of 4-NA. At the same time, formate ions were oxidized to
CO2– radicals by photogenerated holes with high oxidative potential. New-born
CO2-radicals would fast reduce 4-NA to PPD. Therefore enhanced photocatalytic activity for reducing 4-NA to PPD was attributed to the synergistic effect between CQDs loading and Zn2+ ions doping. A schematic illustration for photocatalytic reduction process of 4-NA over CQDs/ZnCdS NWs is shown in Fig. 7 .

In summary, CdS NWs, ZnCdS NWs and CQDs/ZnCdS NWs have been synthesized through a scalable solution-phase route. Among them CQDs/ZnCdS NWs showed the best photocatalytic activity. Under visible light illumination, 4-NA could be efficiently reduced to PPD over CQDs/ZnCdS NWs within 6 min in the presence of ammonium formate. The photocatalytic activity of the as-obtained CQDs/ZnCdS NWs is comparable to other photocatalysts reported (Table S1). From experimental results, it was revealed that Zn2+ ions doping increased VB potential of CdS NWs and CQDs loading boosted charge separation and transfer efficiency in the present reaction system. The synergetic effect between CQDs as metal-free co-catalysts and Zn2+ ions doping was responsible for enhanced photocatalytic activity for CQDs/ZnCdS NWs. The as-obtained CQDs/ZnCdS NWs can be widely applied in the field of solar energy conversion except for the photoctalytic synthesis of fine chemical products.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China for Distinguished Young Scholars under Grant No. LR16B010002 and the National Science Foundation of China (51372173, 21673160 and 51420105002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.04.121.
